source_title: AMA #18: Cold Therapy Advice, Skin Health Tips, Motivation, Learning Strategies & More

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: I'm delighted to kick off this premium subscriber AMA. We've expanded our philanthropy to support exciting science on mental health, physical health, and performance. Thanks to you, we now have three dollar-for-dollar donor matches. For every dollar from the premium channel supporting research, we get $3 donated to match it—a 4X effect. We're supporting work on deliberate heat exposure to treat depression, goal-setting at NYU, and immune-nervous system interactions. As the data come in, we'll relay findings and translate them to protocols.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: Robert asks: is there any way to repair thinning skin as we age? I'm 77, and in the last few years, the skin on my arms has gotten noticeably thinner. Sun protection is critical—excessive sun exposure will age skin more rapidly. That's categorically true. Use a physical barrier like hats and long sleeves, or mineral-based sunscreens with zinc oxide or titanium dioxide up to 25%. Chemical-based sunscreens have some controversy around endocrine disruption, so if you're using them frequently all summer, go with mineral-based formulas.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: To improve skin elasticity and appearance: ingesting collagen proteins at 15 to 30 grams per day with vitamin C shows moderate evidence for improving skin smoothness and plumpness. Red light exposure for 10 to 15 minutes per day, five days per week, also shows moderate improvement. Dermatologist-supported approaches include prescription retinoids and laser resurfacing—both can dramatically improve skin appearance and reduce certain skin cancer risks. Apply a high-quality, non-fragranced moisturizer regularly. Eat a low-inflammatory diet with essential fatty acids, fruits, vegetables, and fiber.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: Jen Shaw asks: how cold does the water need to be for cold therapy to be effective? Make the water just cold enough that you feel uncomfortable and want to get out, but you can safely stay in. I don't obsess over exact temperature. For me, 45 to 50 degrees Fahrenheit works well. Stay in until you adapt—anywhere from one to three minutes. Never do breath work prior to getting into very cold water; blowing off carbon dioxide limits your gasp reflex and can be dangerous. Have somebody monitor you if possible.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: Katie asks about self-motivation to start a new routine. Be very careful who you announce your goals to. I believe in a contract with yourself—write down the goal, check off each day you complete it, sign it like a contract. Stating goals to the world often wears off because people's support creates a mindset that you could do it anytime. Put away your phone, turn it off, put it in another room. The work itself should become the reward. When you sign off that you did the work, that's the reward—a closed loop system of motivation.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: Lou asks: what can you do if you're not getting enough REM sleep? Add 10 to 30 minutes to your sleep schedule in the morning. Get a bigger surge of adrenaline early in the day through deliberate cold exposure or exercise—this can increase REM sleep that same night. Do a 10 to 30 minute non-sleep deep rest protocol in the morning. If you don't get enough REM sleep one night, there's a REM rebound effect—you'll get more REM sleep the next night, provided you allow sufficient sleep and avoid caffeine late in the day or alcohol.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: Danielle asks: the top three most impactful things schools could do to raise student capacity for learning. First, get kids to sleep enough—neuroplasticity occurs during deep sleep and REM sleep. Second, start each learning session with five minutes of meditation or non-sleep deep rest. Wendy Suzuki's laboratory showed even 13 minutes per day significantly improves working memory, focus, and decreases stress. Third, introduce micro gaps—brief 10-second pauses during information delivery allow rapid replay of information at 20 to 30 times normal speed in the brain, generating more repetitions. Fourth, include physical activity, especially cardiovascular training, immediately before learning.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: Alec asks about Shilajit for cognitive function and testosterone. Shilajit is a mineral pitch from the Himalayas. There's some data showing small increases in follicle stimulating hormone, sperm quality, and testosterone, but I wouldn't place Shilajit high on the list. Far better ways to support testosterone: make sure body fat percentage is neither too high nor too low, get an extra 30 to 60 minutes of sleep each night, exercise properly. If you want to explore supplementation, start with Tongkat Ali or Fadogia before Shilajit.
